narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.30 and an LSAT score of 27, your profile also includes strong family support from an upper-middle income background and plans to attend law school full-time. The admissions model evaluated your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, predicting a 72% chance that you would fail, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates expected to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which stood out as the most influential factor pushing the model strongly toward forecasting bar exam failure—far below the typical student's average decile of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school raised significant concerns about your readiness for the rigors ahead. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing, as it's modestly above the average of 3.21 and suggests solid prior achievement. Similarly, your upper-middle family income bracket provided a mild positive nudge toward success, likely reflecting resources that help many students like you navigate challenges. Compared to typical students, your low Year 3 decile was a stark outlier dragging the overall outlook down, overshadowing those helpful elements despite your full-time commitment aligning with the norm for over 90% of applicants. While your LSAT score of 27 is notably below the average of 36.7, it wasn't among the top drivers here. This combination means the model saw your academic trajectory as a red flag for bar passage, even with your personal strengths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, seeking ways to improve late-stage grades through additional coursework or tutoring, and highlighting any extenuating circumstances in a future application. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the prediction in your favor, as even small improvements in key areas like decile rankings often make a big difference. You're clearly motivated—use this feedback to refine your path and target schools with more holistic reviews.
